title: Novartis heart drug fails in Phase 3
summary: Novartis announced that its experimental heart drug serelaxin (RLX030) failed a Phase 3 trial in patients with acute heart failure, failing to extend the time before heart failure worsened or death from cardiovascular complications. This is a significant setback for a drug expected to generate at least $1 billion annually. Novartis plans to evaluate the best way closing remaining studies, though three others are still underway.
analysis_notes: Article dates to March 2017; serelaxin was a long-shot candidate for acute heart failure. While not recent, the analysis notes context on Novartis's pipeline at the time including Entresto and Ilaris as revenue drivers.

title: AbbVie, Genmab, with new data, prep FDA application for dual-acting cancer drug
summary: AbbVie and Genmab reported that epcoritamab, a dual-acting bispecific CD3xCD20 antibody, produced treatment responses in 63% of patients with relapsed/refractory large B-cell lymphoma (LBCL) across 157 participants. The partners plan to submit the Phase 1/2 trial data to the FDA for consideration of approval. Epcoritamab's safety profile -- including a milder cytokine release syndrome compared to CAR-T therapies and potential steroid-free management in low-severity cases -- positions it competitively against Roche's mosunetuzumab (68% response rate with Polivy) and other bispecifics from Regeneron and Johnson & Johnson.
analysis_notes: Article is dated April 14, 2022; data may be stale by current date (July 2026). The article content was minimal but sufficient to extract key findings -- primarily a press-release-style brief with standard Dive Insight commentary on bispecific antibody competitive landscape. Response rate of 63% for epcoritamab is noted as competitive in LBCL, though the trial lacked a control arm and FDA review would likely result only in accelerated approval pending confirmatory controlled trials.

title: AbbVie wagers more than $1B on Gilgamesh's psychedelic drug
summary: AbbVie has agreed to acquire the psychedelic compound bretisilocin (GM-2505) from Gilgamesh Pharmaceuticals in a deal worth up to $1.2 billion, positioning the pharmaceutical giant as a major player in the emerging psychedelic drug space. The acquisition follows positive Phase 2a clinical trial results for the drug, which is being developed to treat major depressive disorder and works similarly to psilocybin-based therapies.
analysis_notes: AbbVie's prior collaboration with Gilgamesh from 2024 had a $65M upfront payment plus up to $1.95B in potential milestone payments; the new acquisition consolidates those rights into a new spinoff company, Gilgamesh Pharma Inc., which retains other programs including an iboga-derived analog and an experimental schizophrenia medication.

title: On second try, Acadia drug gets nod from European regulators
summary: The EU's CHMP committee recommended marketing authorization for Acadia Pharmaceuticals' Daybue in treating Rett syndrome after initially rejecting it, with a final EC decision expected within 67 days. The same committee voted to revoke Amgen's Tavneos approval due to serious side effects and approved several other drug expansions including Eli Lilly's Jaypirca for CLL and additional indications for Datroway, Tecvayli, and Opzelura.

title: Acelyrin follows Kenvue with largest IPO for a biotech startup since 2021
summary: Acelyrin raised $540 million in its IPO by selling 30 million shares at $18 each, marking the largest biotech startup offering since Sana Biotechnology's $588 million raise in February 2021. Shares surged above $23 by Monday following Friday's Nasdaq debut under ticker SLRN. The company's lead drug candidate, izokibep—a Phase-3 IL-17A inhibitor for inflammatory conditions including psoriatic arthritis and axial spondyloarthritis—is licensed from Swedish developer Affibody and represents the kind of late-stage asset that investor-ready public markets now demand.
analysis_notes: Article published May 4, 2023; context reflects pre-recent IPO market conditions. The piece provides detailed IPO pricing, pipeline status, and industry sentiment analysis via Baird quotes.

title: ADA '26: Lilly's dominance, Pfizer's 'foundational' drug and Roche's 'me-too' option
summary: At the American Diabetes Association's 2026 meeting, Eli Lilly reinforced its obesity drug market leadership with retatrutide, a triple-agonist therapy that produced 28% body weight loss (approximately 70 pounds) in the Phase 3 TRIUMPH-1 study over 80 weeks. Pfizer presented Phase 2b data for berobenatide, acquired via its $10 billion Metsera deal, showing 16% placebo-adjusted weight loss but with tolerability concerns including a 21% discontinuation rate in one monthly-dose trial. Roche's enicepatide was viewed by analysts as largely undifferentiated and late to market compared to established competitors.
analysis_notes: Article provides substantive clinical and commercial analysis of three major obesity drug programs at ADA 2026. Key data points include retatrutide's 28% body weight loss (comparable to bariatric surgery), berobenatide's 16% efficacy with higher discontinuation rates, and enicepatide being characterized as 'me-too' and 'undifferentiated.' The article positions the obesity drug market around evolving competitive dynamics beyond Lilly/Novo duopoly.

title: Akeso, Summit drug extends survival in closely watched lung cancer trial
summary: Ivonescimab, a bispecific PD-1/VEGF inhibitor developed by Akeso and Summit Therapeutics, demonstrated a statistically significant 34% relative risk reduction in overall death compared to chemotherapy alone in the HARMONi-6 trial for advanced non-small cell lung cancer (median OS: 28 vs. 24 months). The results reinforce the potential of the PD-1/VEGF inhibitor class but fell slightly short of some investor expectations; Summit shares fell up to 12% following the announcement. A global HARMONi-3 head-to-head trial against Merck's Keytruda is expected to report later this year.

title: Alcon buys majority stake in IPO-seeking cell therapy startup Aurion
summary: Alcon has acquired a majority stake in Aurion Biotech, ending months of legal disputes between Alcon and shareholder Deerfield Management over the company's direction after Aurion had filed for an IPO in January 2025. The deal brings Arnaud Lacoste (CFO) as the new CEO replacing Greg Kunst, with plans to advance AURN001 into Phase 3 development later this year using Alcon's global resources.

title: Allergan, Mohawk tribe ask for Supreme Court review of scrutinized patent case
summary: Allergan and the St. Regis Mohawk Tribe petitioned the U.S. Supreme Court to review a Federal Circuit ruling that tribal sovereign immunity cannot protect Allergan's dry eye drug Restasis from patent challenges by generic rivals. The unusual 2017 deal transferred Restasis patents to the tribe, which then invoked sovereign immunity to dismiss inter partes review proceedings — but the PTAB and Federal Circuit both rejected the strategy.

title: Allogene to shift strategy in bid to reposition CAR-T
summary: Allogene Therapeutics is deprioritizing two advanced Phase 2 trials for its off-the-shelf CAR-T therapy cema-cel in late-line lymphoma and restructuring to extend its operating runway into 2026. Instead, the company will pivot resources toward a new Phase 2 trial (ALPHA3) testing cema-cel as an adjuvant after R-CHOP chemotherapy in approximately 230 advanced large-B-cell lymphoma patients at risk of relapse. Allogene also plans to explore its cell therapy approach in autoimmune diseases starting next year, though no specific indication has been announced.

title: Alveus launches with $160M to advance MariTide-like obesity drug
summary: Alveus Therapeutics emerged from stealth on Jan. 8, 2026 with $160M in Series A funding to advance its lead drug ALV-100, a fusion protein that stimulates GLP-1R and blocks GIPR, mirroring Amgen's MariTide approach. The company is also developing ALV-200, an amylin-targeting peptide for weight loss expected to enter FDAIND-enabling studies. Management claims preclinical data shows lean body mass preservation superior to existing GLP-1 therapies.
analysis_notes: Content quality is solid — this was a fully rendered article (unlike most BioPharma Dive URLs that return empty shells). Notable gap: no disclosed dosing regimen, timeline for Phase 2 initiation, or specific preclinical data figures in the text. Competition landscape with MariTide is described but Amgen's Phase 2 results are characterized as 'skeptical' without detail.

title: New Amgen obesity drug data disappoint Wall Street
summary: Amgen's Phase 2 results for MariTide, an experimental obesity drug targeting GLP-1 and GIP receptors, showed up to 16% body weight loss over a year but fell short of prior reported figures after accounting for study dropouts. The data, presented at the American Diabetes Association meeting and published in NEJM, also revealed high rates of vomiting leading to discontinuation, triggering a 4% drop in Amgen's stock price. Wall Street is divided: some analysts view the tolerability issues as commercially fatal while others see the Phase 3 three-step dosing approach as potentially resolving the problem.

title: Under-the-skin Tepezza comparable to infused version in key study, Amgen says
summary: Amgen reported that a subcutaneous (on-body injector) formulation of Tepezza met both primary and secondary endpoints in its Phase 3 trial for thyroid eye disease, with 77% of treated patients showing meaningful reduction in eye bulging versus 20% on placebo. Research chief Jay Bradner described the IV-like efficacy as comparable to the existing intravenous infusion version Tepezza OBI is marketed under, which could expand patient access by eliminating the need for infusion centers.
analysis_notes: Article content is substantial and high-quality. Tepezza generates ~$2B annual revenue; subcutaneous version may help defend market share against Viridian's elegrobart (Phase 3) and veligrotug (expected FDA decision by June 2026). Competitors face pricing/market access challenges given the established incumbent position.
